jokes.text: Hey-hey, you're pregnant and it's not mine!
jokes.setup: Home pregnancy test product from Krusty's company
jokes.punchline: The test announces pregnancy while simultaneously denying paternity responsibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Krusty character moment - combining his sleazy persona with corporate irresponsibility. The absurdity of a pregnancy test denying paternity is genuinely clever.

jokes.text: Repackage them as coffee stirrers and sell them in the Philippines.
jokes.setup: Defective pregnancy tests need disposal
jokes.punchline: Cynical rebranding to dump faulty products on developing country
jokes.explanation: Dark corporate satire that fits Krusty's unethical character perfectly. Shows his complete lack of moral boundaries.

jokes.text: What's that food we never put in any of our meals? Vegetables? Yeah, those guys.
jokes.setup: Need to make healthier burger option
jokes.punchline: Krusty treats vegetables like foreign entities he barely recognizes
jokes.explanation: Great character beat showing Krusty's complete disconnect from healthy eating. 'Yeah, those guys' is perfectly dismissive.

jokes.text: What are those unhealthy he-she sounding things? Uh, trans fats?
jokes.setup: Krusty trying to identify unhealthy ingredients
jokes.punchline: He can't remember 'trans fats' and describes them by sound rather than meaning
jokes.explanation: Shows Krusty's ignorance about nutrition while creating wordplay. Moderately clever but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Spray a little nicotine juice on the kids' meals. Get 'em hooked.
jokes.setup: Looking for ways to increase business
jokes.punchline: Casually suggests addicting children to nicotine through food
jokes.explanation: Peak Krusty evil - treating child addiction as a business strategy. Strong character integration but familiar territory for the show.

jokes.text: It was worth it to be on TV! Put our dying screams in the promo!
jokes.setup: Reality TV show gone wrong with people in danger
jokes.punchline: Even while dying, participants want their suffering used for promotion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of reality TV culture where people prioritize fame over safety. The enthusiasm for using their death throes in marketing is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: The real drowningest catch is man.
jokes.setup: Parody of 'Deadliest Catch' fishing show
jokes.punchline: Philosophical observation using the show's signature awkward phrasing
jokes.explanation: Decent parody line that captures the pseudo-profound tone of reality TV. Good economy but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Made from 100% wheat-fed barley.
jokes.setup: Promoting new healthy burger
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical ingredient description that sounds healthy but makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing corporate nonsense speak. Functional but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: Finally, a hamburger for the Hindus. By the tusks of great Ganesh, now no one can tell I am not American.
jokes.setup: Apu excited about vegetarian burger option
jokes.punchline: Thinks eating a burger will make him seem American while using distinctly Indian expressions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Apu character moment showing his desire to assimilate while being completely unconscious of his Indian mannerisms. The irony is well-executed.

jokes.text: Look at me! I'm saving the Earth! Where's my Nobel prize?
jokes.setup: Lisa eating the supposedly healthy burger
jokes.punchline: Immediately demands recognition for minor environmental action
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa character beat showing her earnestness and slight pretension. The instant expectation of a Nobel Prize is appropriately absurd for her age.

jokes.text: Those barley burgers were tainted! Why did I eat 12 of them? Why?!
jokes.setup: Homer sick from bad burgers
jokes.punchline: The absurd quantity he consumed despite them being disgusting
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer gluttony joke. The number 12 is perfectly excessive and his anguished 'Why?!' is appropriately dramatic. Pure character comedy.

jokes.text: Taken! Occupied! Hurling!
jokes.setup: Students rushing to bathroom stalls
jokes.punchline: Final stall claimed not by someone using it normally but someone vomiting
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure building to the gross-out punchline. Excellent economy with just three words painting the whole scene.

jokes.text: If I die, I want you to take over... Really? ...the search committee for a new superintendent.
jokes.setup: Chalmers appears to be giving Skinner final important instructions
jokes.punchline: It's just a bureaucratic task, and Skinner's hopeful reaction is crushed
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect character work. Skinner's hopeful 'Really?' makes the deflation even funnier. Great structure and timing.

jokes.text: Third-grade math scores are holding steady.
jokes.setup: Skinner asked to say soothing things to dying Chalmers
jokes.punchline: He offers mundane administrative updates as comfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner character beat - he literally can't think outside his administrative mindset even in an intimate moment. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What happened is, a big ol' rat got caught up in the grinders there, so my wife, Dagny, took a hose to it.
jokes.setup: News reporter asking about food contamination
jokes.punchline: Farmer casually describes horrific food safety violation as routine maintenance
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for the Norwegian farmer's matter-of-fact attitude toward gross situations. Sets up the contamination plot effectively.

jokes.text: Well, where there's barley, there's rats, you know. Now, when will you be starting the interview?
jokes.setup: Reporter asking if barley is safe to eat again
jokes.punchline: Farmer casually accepts rats as inevitable, then reveals he didn't know interview was happening
jokes.explanation: Double beat joke - first the casual acceptance of rat contamination, then the obliviousness about being on live TV. Good character work for simple Norwegian farmer.

jokes.text: You hear that, Bart? He called me 'sir.' That means he knows I'm better than him.
jokes.setup: Norwegian worker addresses Homer respectfully
jokes.punchline: Homer interprets basic politeness as acknowledgment of superiority
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment showing his need to feel superior and complete misunderstanding of social interactions. Good character integration.

jokes.text: A lemon version of barley-ade?
jokes.setup: Homer offers lemonade to workers
jokes.punchline: Norwegian translates it into their barley-obsessed worldview
jokes.explanation: Mild character beat establishing Norwegians' barley obsession. Functional setup for later jokes but not particularly funny on its own.

jokes.text: I'm glad you've come here to take all the jobs we don't want to do. Can't have too much of a good thing.
jokes.setup: Homer welcoming immigrant workers
jokes.punchline: Immediately establishes the ironic premise that will be reversed later
jokes.explanation: More setup than punchline, establishing the ironic attitude that will be reversed. Gets rewatch bonus for the dramatic irony.

jokes.text: That I do! But he doesn't stay long. No, I don't!
jokes.setup: Marge explaining Ned drops by unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Ned appears instantly to demonstrate the behavior, then leaves just as quickly
jokes.explanation: Excellent physical comedy timing inferred from dialogue. Ned's immediate appearance and departure perfectly demonstrates the behavior being described. Great economy and structure.

jokes.text: Mom says I can have ice cream for breakfast. Oh, she's still here. Mom, can I have ice cream for breakfast? No way, mister.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to manipulate new nanny with false claims
jokes.punchline: His manipulation fails immediately because Marge is right there
jokes.explanation: Good Bart characterization showing his opportunistic nature and poor planning. The deflated 'Oh, she's still here' is well-timed.

jokes.text: Just like the ones Joe Piscopo and Ethan Hawke left their wives for. But hands off my Homie.
jokes.setup: Marge introducing new nanny Inga
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific celebrity references followed by territorial warning
jokes.explanation: The Joe Piscopo reference is oddly specific and dated, which makes it funnier. Shows Marge's pop culture awareness and protective instincts.

jokes.text: Ja, she has the same beautiful face as my great-uncle Magnus when he lay in the coffin after his trolley accident.
jokes.setup: Torbjorn describing how he met Selma
jokes.punchline: Compares her beauty to a dead relative in a coffin
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and specific comparison that perfectly captures both Norwegian stoicism and Selma's unfortunate appearance. The specificity of 'trolley accident' makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Hey, Homer, your blubber would light our lamps for a year. I zing the oaf good? You zing the oaf great.
jokes.setup: Norwegian making joke about Homer's weight
jokes.punchline: Asking for approval of his insult and getting encouragement
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing Norwegian trying to fit in through insult comedy. The validation-seeking makes it more endearing than mean.

jokes.text: This is Tennessee Titans country!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy seeing Minnesota Vikings merchandise
jokes.punchline: Nerdy character suddenly shows passionate sports territoriality
jokes.explanation: Good character subversion - Comic Book Guy showing unexpected sports passion. The specificity helps.

jokes.text: Step aside, Nor-wads. Watch how the natives ride sky.
jokes.setup: Bart showing off to Norwegian kids at playground
jokes.punchline: His arrogant demonstration presumably goes wrong (implied by following sounds)
jokes.explanation: Good Bart characterization showing his cockiness and casual xenophobia. 'Ride sky' is amusingly awkward phrasing. Setup for physical comedy.

jokes.text: A-fer-gan-nerkin! A-fer-gan-nerkin!
jokes.setup: Norwegian sneezing in hospital
jokes.punchline: Elaborate, foreign-sounding sneeze that sounds like gibberish
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing foreign stereotype humor. The elaborate sneeze sound is memorable but relies on 'foreign = funny' which is somewhat cheap.

jokes.text: I can't read this sloopy-bloopy writing.
jokes.setup: Homer given Norwegian medical forms
jokes.punchline: Describes foreign language with childish nonsense words
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer characterization - reducing unfamiliar writing to baby talk. 'Sloopy-bloopy' is appropriately childish vocabulary for him.

jokes.text: Typical-- foreigners clogging up the system. I hurt my back unclogging your septic system. Sven, can you stop by tomorrow? It's clogged again.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about foreigners clogging the healthcare system
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks the foreigner to unclog his actual septic system
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony showing Homer's hypocrisy perfectly. The wordplay between 'clogging the system' and literal plumbing is clever, and the immediate contradiction is beautifully executed.

jokes.text: Na-chin-na-hart! Na-chin-na-hart!
jokes.setup: Catching Ogdenvillian cold
jokes.punchline: Different elaborate sneeze sound, showing he's caught their 'foreign' sneeze
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier sneeze gag with escalation. Functional but diminishing returns on the concept.

jokes.text: Well, you were a baby once. Does that mean you still like milk and hugs? Yes, I'd like both right now.
jokes.setup: Homer making argument against Marge's immigration point
jokes.punchline: His rhetorical question backfires when Marge sincerely wants comfort
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection where Homer's attempt at clever rhetoric reveals Marge's emotional needs. Sweet character moment with solid structure.

jokes.text: Hey, I ain't got no problem with them. They pay in cash, they keep it clean, and their mythology is rich and enchanting.
jokes.setup: Moe defending Norwegian customers
jokes.punchline: Escalates from practical concerns to unexpectedly sophisticated cultural appreciation
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moe character moment showing his surprising depth. The escalation from mundane bar owner concerns to genuine cultural appreciation is perfectly unexpected.

jokes.text: Hey, Moe, what is that hoity-toity beer they're drinking? Oh, I don't sell beer no more. I only serve Aquavit, the Norwegian caraway-scented liqueur.
jokes.setup: Barney asking about Norwegian drinks
jokes.punchline: Moe has completely abandoned beer for specialized foreign alcohol
jokes.explanation: Shows how thoroughly the Norwegian influence has taken over Springfield. Moe completely abandoning beer is a significant change for his character.

jokes.text: That stuff will open up a can of 'oh my goodness' on you.
jokes.setup: Warning Homer about strong Norwegian liquor
jokes.punchline: Replaces profanity in common expression with polite phrase
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay substituting family-friendly language in tough-guy warning. Good economy and memorable phrasing.

jokes.text: I share your xylophobia. Dad, you mean xenophobia. Xylophobia would be a fear of xylophones. I am afraid of xylophones. It's the music you hear when skeletons are dancing.
jokes.setup: Mayor incorrectly using xylophobia instead of xenophobia
jokes.punchline: When corrected, he doubles down with absurd but oddly logical reasoning
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from malapropism to absurd but weirdly logical phobia. 'The music you hear when skeletons are dancing' is perfectly absurd and highly quotable. Excellent structure and originality.

jokes.text: Springfield will ban immigrants and xylophones.
jokes.setup: Mayor's confusion about xylophobia/xenophobia
jokes.punchline: Official policy bans both immigrants and musical instruments
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that takes the xylophone joke to its logical absurd conclusion. The pairing of immigrants and xylophones in official policy is brilliantly ridiculous.

jokes.text: Well, he's too far, Lou. So? So what am I, Superman?
jokes.setup: Lou suggesting they pursue fleeing immigrant
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's lazy excuse comparing basic police work to superhero abilities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum characterization showing his complete incompetence and tendency to find excuses. The Superman comparison for basic running is appropriately absurd.

jokes.text: Eddie loves his lobster and port wine, but it comes with a price.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Eddie is out with gout
jokes.punchline: Sophisticated expensive tastes causing rich person's disease in working-class cop
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Eddie has unexpectedly upscale tastes. The contrast between his job and his expensive dietary habits is amusing.

jokes.text: vigilantes who would agree to do our job for the adrenaline rush that comes from having a tiny taste of authority over your fellow man
jokes.setup: Need for border patrol help
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest psychological analysis of vigilante motivation
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary disguised as character dialogue. Lou's insight into human nature is unexpectedly sophisticated.

jokes.text: And all we're paying 'em is bullets and beer.
jokes.setup: Recruiting vigilantes to patrol border
jokes.punchline: Payment consists entirely of weapons and alcohol
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about arming vigilantes with the worst possible combination of tools. Shows Wiggum's complete lack of judgment about public safety.

jokes.text: The Klan? Well, there are no bad ideas, but let's keep trying. The Nazis? Okay, you stop trying.
jokes.setup: Group trying to name their vigilante organization
jokes.punchline: Immediately suggests worst possible historical groups, with escalating inappropriateness
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with dark historical references. Moe's diplomatic 'no bad ideas' followed by immediate shutdown is well-timed.

jokes.text: My code name will be 'Screaming Eagle.' Yours will be 'Ground Losers.'
jokes.setup: Moe assigning radio code names
jokes.punchline: Gives himself heroic name while insulting everyone else
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe characterization showing his selfishness and casual cruelty. The contrast between 'Screaming Eagle' and 'Ground Losers' is perfectly mean-spirited.

jokes.text: C-c-cold. S-s-so very cold.
jokes.setup: Balloon patrol duty
jokes.punchline: Pathetic radio transmission revealing misery of assignment
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy. The chattering delivery effectively conveys the miserable conditions.

jokes.text: That's not a tree, Chief. That's a street sign. You just looped your belt around it when you were changing your pants.
jokes.setup: Wiggum claiming he was handcuffed to a tree by immigrant
jokes.punchline: Lou reveals Wiggum's incompetent mistake and embarrassing circumstances
jokes.explanation: Excellent Wiggum incompetence joke with multiple layers - wrong object identification, self-inflicted 'capture,' and pants-changing context adds embarrassment.

jokes.text: Ja. Ja. Maggie's first words are in Ogdenvillese! Now do you see, Marge? Now do you see why we must build that fence? Build it, Homie! Make it as tall as the sky and deeper than Hell.
jokes.setup: Baby Maggie saying 'ja' like the Norwegians
jokes.punchline: Homer's extreme reaction and Marge's complete reversal to supporting massive wall construction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how minor cultural influence triggers extreme overreaction. Marge's complete flip from reasonable to extremist is well-executed.

jokes.text: So my girlfriend puts all these pillows on the bed. And you have to like take them all off at night and put them all back on every morning. And if the little one is in back of the big one, oh, God help you.
jokes.setup: Carl complaining about girlfriend's decorative pillows
jokes.punchline: Escalating frustration with specific pillow arrangement rules
jokes.explanation: Relatable observational humor about relationship dynamics. The escalation to 'God help you' for pillow placement is appropriately dramatic.

jokes.text: I had trouble finding a four-button cardigan, so I bought a three-button one and sewed the extra button on myself. This baby was a five-buttoner, but I took a button off.
jokes.setup: Men bonding over clothing preferences
jokes.punchline: Elaborate efforts to achieve specific button counts on cardigans
jokes.explanation: Absurd but oddly endearing bonding over extremely specific clothing modifications. Shows how men find connection through shared obsessions.

jokes.text: Do ya miss us? Ja, we miss you. That's why we build the door.
jokes.setup: Homer and Sven talking through the border wall
jokes.punchline: Norwegians built a door in the wall they were hired to construct
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual gag with perfect economy. The practical Norwegian solution undermining the entire premise of the wall is brilliantly subversive. Great character work showing their competence and kindness.

jokes.text: All right, boys, round 'em up... and throw them the swingingest soiree they've ever seen!
jokes.setup: Wiggum ordering what sounds like an arrest
jokes.punchline: It's actually an order to throw a party
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with classic police language subverted to party planning. 'Swingingest soiree' is appropriately over-the-top language for Wiggum.
